steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage makes two moves: (1) it warns that copying Luang Prabang's architecture may not increase walking elsewhere, and (2) it explains why, through Alfonzo's argument that walking decisions depend on complex, multi-factor contexts. The main purpose ties both moves together.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "To describe the effect of human-scaled architecture on any given city's walkability.": treats architecture as the central topic, but the passage uses it only as one example of a replicable feature. The passage argues against relying on architecture as a solution, not in favor of describing its effects. ✗
  • "To explain why it is challenging to compare the amount of pedestrian traffic in different cities.": the passage is not about comparing pedestrian traffic counts across cities. Luang Prabang is mentioned to illustrate why replication fails, not to be compared with other cities. ✗
  • "To present a claim about how individuals' decision-making about walking can be improved.": the passage describes the complexity of the problem — it does not propose or advocate any improvement. Alfonzo's argument is diagnostic, not prescriptive. ✗
  • "To discuss the difficulty of identifying a reliable way to increase walking in any given city.": captures both moves: "difficult" aligns with the multi-factor complexity Alfonzo describes, and "reliable way to increase walking" matches the concern that replicating Luang Prabang's features may be insufficient. ✓
That matches the correct answer — D is correct.
